Write a pricing A/B test plan. Compare the current $49 tier to a new $79 Pro tier. Cover the statistical setup, metrics, and duration.
hint: Requiring a guardrail metric and flagging the ethical consideration of simultaneous price differences produces a test design that a legal and finance team can approve.

title: Write quick weekly status update
originalPrompt: Write a weekly product status update for the leadership team.
options: Write a weekly product status update. Max 5 bullets, max 15 words each. Sections: shipped this week (2 bullets), blocked (1 bullet), decision needed (1 bullet), metric highlight (1 bullet). Plain language, no filler.

Write a weekly status update for the leadership team covering what the product team shipped this week, any blockers we're dealing with, decisions that leadership needs to make, and a key metric from our dashboard. Keep it concise and easy to read. Leadership is busy and we want to give them the most important information without overwhelming them with detail.
Weekly update: shipped X, blocked on Y, need decision on Z.
hint: Assigning a section role to each bullet with a word cap produces a status update that takes 30 seconds to read and drives the right leadership conversations.

title: Write platform strategy memo
originalPrompt: Write a strategy memo about turning our product into a platform. Leadership needs to understand the opportunity.
options: Write a 300-word strategy memo on transitioning a B2B SaaS product to a platform model. Structure: strategic rationale (why now—2 market signals), what changes (3 platform capabilities to build: API, marketplace, partner ecosystem), 3-year revenue model impact (ecosystem vs direct), execution risk (top 2 risks with mitigations), and the ask from leadership. Audience: CEO and board. Tone: analytical, not visionary hype.
Write a strategy memo about becoming a platform. Explain the market opportunity, what we need to build, the revenue impact, and what risks we need to manage.
Write a memo on our platform strategy. Cover why we're doing this, the three capabilities we need, financial impact, and key risks.
hint: Requiring an explicit 'ask from leadership' section and banning visionary hype ensures the memo is a decision document, not a thought piece.

title: Write AI feature ethics review
originalPrompt: A PM needs to conduct an ethics review of an AI feature before it ships.
options: Write an AI feature ethics review framework for a recommendation algorithm that personalises content for B2C users. Evaluate: (1) bias risk (training data representation, affected groups), (2) transparency (can users understand why they see content?), (3) consent and data use (what data is used, opt-out options), (4) harm potential (filter bubble risk, addictive design), (5) audit mechanism (how will we monitor post-launch). For each: risk level (high/medium/low) and required mitigation before GA.
Write an ethics review for our AI recommendation feature. Cover bias, transparency, privacy, and potential harms. Include mitigations.
Conduct an ethics review of our recommendation algorithm. Evaluate bias, user transparency, data consent, and harm risks. Rate each and describe the mitigations needed before launch.
hint: Requiring a 'required mitigation before GA' field per dimension creates a gating mechanism that can block a launch, making the ethics review structurally impactful.

title: Write feature brief for design kickoff
originalPrompt: Write a brief for designers for our new feature. They need to understand what we're building before they start designing.
options: Write a feature design brief for a bulk email notification settings feature. Include: problem statement (1 sentence), user goal, out-of-scope items (2-3), constraints (technical and UX), success metrics, and open questions for the design team. Max 250 words. Audience: product designer and UX researcher starting discovery.
Write a design brief for our notification settings feature. Cover the problem, user needs, constraints, and metrics.
Write a brief for designers working on bulk notification settings. Include the user story, constraints, what not to build, and how we'll measure success.
hint: Including an 'open questions for the design team' section invites designers into the problem-solving process rather than handing them a specification to execute.

title: Write research repository tagging taxonomy
originalPrompt: We have a lot of research notes and need a way to organise them. Help me create a tagging system.
options: Design a research repository tagging taxonomy for a B2B SaaS product team with 3 researchers and 2 years of accumulated studies. Define tag categories: (1) product area (list 5-8 relevant tags), (2) insight type (pain point, behaviour, motivation, mental model), (3) user segment, (4) data type (qual/quant/mixed), (5) confidence level (validated/directional/anecdotal). Include a tagging guide: when each tag applies and how to handle multi-tag conflicts. Output as a taxonomy document.
Create a tagging system for our research repository. Include tags for product area, insight type, user segment, and confidence level.
Design a research repository taxonomy. Define tag categories for our studies, explain when to apply each tag, and how to handle cases where multiple tags apply.
hint: Including a 'how to handle multi-tag conflicts' rule is the difference between a taxonomy researchers use consistently and one that degrades into chaos over time.

title: Write quarterly product review summary
originalPrompt: Write a quarterly product review summary. Cover what we achieved and what we learned.
options: Write a Q3 product review summary for an internal team meeting. Sections: (1) goals we set (list 3), (2) goals we hit/missed with a brief reason, (3) top 3 learnings, (4) how this changes Q4 priorities. Max 300 words. Tone: honest, forward-looking. No spin.
Write a quarterly product review. Cover our goals, what we achieved, the main learnings, and what we're changing for next quarter.
Write a Q3 product review summary. Include our goals, outcomes, learnings, and updated priorities. Keep it honest and concise.
hint: Requiring 'a brief reason' for each hit/miss and connecting learnings to Q4 priorities produces a review that drives change rather than just recapping history.

title: Design strategic partnership evaluation
originalPrompt: A product VP needs to evaluate three potential strategic technology partnerships before recommending one to the CEO.
options: Create a partnership evaluation framework for 3 potential technology partners for a B2B SaaS company. Evaluate each partner on: strategic fit (alignment with 3-year vision), technical integration complexity (low/medium/high), customer value add (how many customers benefit, average ARR impact), exclusivity risk, and exit cost. Build a weighted scoring matrix (weights must sum to 100%). Output: ranked recommendation with a 2-sentence rationale per partner.
Evaluate 3 technology partnerships for our SaaS company. Compare them on strategic fit, integration complexity, customer value, and risk.
Write a partnership evaluation comparing 3 technology vendors. Score each on strategic alignment, integration effort, customer impact, and risk. Recommend one with rationale.
hint: Requiring an exclusivity risk and exit cost assessment prevents the company from discovering a lock-in problem after the partnership contract is signed.

title: Write accessibility audit checklist
originalPrompt: A design systems team needs an accessibility checklist for designers to self-audit their work before engineering handoff.
options: Write an accessibility self-audit checklist for product designers before engineering handoff. Group items by WCAG principle: Perceivable (colour contrast, alt text, non-colour indicators), Operable (keyboard access, focus order, target size), Understandable (labels, error messages, reading order), Robust (component names, ARIA roles). Each item should be a binary yes/no question. Max 20 items total. Note WCAG 2.1 AA criterion reference per item.
Write an accessibility checklist for designers. Cover contrast, keyboard navigation, labels, and ARIA. Make it easy to self-audit.
Create a design accessibility checklist. Group by WCAG principles, include colour contrast, keyboard, and semantic markup. Reference WCAG criteria for each item.
hint: Grouping by WCAG principle rather than component type and requiring a WCAG criterion reference per item produces a checklist that also educates designers on the standard.

title: Write grid system documentation
originalPrompt: Document our grid system. Designers and developers need to know how it works.
options: Write grid system documentation for a design system. Cover: (1) grid type (12-column), (2) breakpoint-specific column count, gutter, and margin values for mobile/tablet/desktop, (3) do/don't examples for column spanning, (4) how to use the grid in Figma (auto-layout vs grid overlay), (5) CSS class or variable names. Output as a reference doc for designers and engineers.
Document our 12-column grid system. Include the column counts, gutters, and margins for each breakpoint. Add usage guidance.
Write grid documentation. Cover the column grid, gutters, margins at different breakpoints, and how to use it in Figma and code.
hint: Requiring both Figma usage instructions and CSS variable names in the same doc prevents the common problem of designers and engineers using different grid implementations.

title: Write Storybook component story description
originalPrompt: Write a description for a component story in Storybook.
options: Write a Storybook component story description for an Alert component. Max 50 words. Include: what the component is, the 4 variants (info/success/warning/error), and one accessibility note. Plain language.

Write a Storybook description for our Alert component. Explain what it is and when to use it. Cover all the variants including informational, success, warning, and error states. Include accessibility guidance for screen reader users and mention any ARIA attributes that are required. The description should help developers and designers understand how to use the component correctly in their work.
Alert component: 4 variants, accessible, for system feedback.
hint: A 50-word cap with a required accessibility note produces a Storybook description that fits the component canvas without scrolling.

title: Write product feedback triage process
originalPrompt: A PM needs a consistent process for triaging incoming product feedback from multiple channels (support tickets, NPS comments, sales calls).
options: Design a product feedback triage process for a B2B SaaS team. Specify: (1) input sources and owners, (2) tagging taxonomy (bug, feature request, UX friction, pricing, churn signal), (3) weekly triage cadence (who attends, time box, output), (4) threshold for escalating a tag to a roadmap item (e.g. 10+ unique customers). Output as a process diagram description and a tag legend.
Write a feedback triage process. Cover how we collect feedback, how we tag it, and how we decide what goes on the roadmap.
Create a product feedback process. Include input sources, tagging categories, a review cadence, and criteria for when feedback becomes a roadmap item.
hint: Specifying a numeric threshold for escalation to roadmap prevents the triage meeting from devolving into subjective debates about what counts as important.

title: Write strategic context for a design sprint
originalPrompt: Write a strategic context brief for a design sprint on reducing time-to-value for new B2B customers.
options: Write a design sprint strategic context brief. Max 100 words. Include: business problem (1 sentence with a metric), sprint focus question (How Might We format), target user and their job-to-be-done, 1 constraint, and the success metric for the sprint outcome.

Write a strategic context document for our design sprint on reducing time-to-value for new customers. Explain the business problem we're trying to solve, including relevant data about our current onboarding metrics. State the sprint focus question in HMW format. Describe the target user and what they're trying to accomplish. List any important constraints the team should work within. Define what success looks like at the end of the sprint.
Sprint brief: reduce onboarding time-to-value. HMW question. Success = faster activation.
hint: A 100-word cap with five required elements forces the sprint brief to contain only the information the team needs to align quickly and diverge productively.
